content: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder of the neuromuscular junction causing fluctuating muscle weakness and fatigability. Incidence 1-9/100,000/year, bimodal distribution (young women 20-40, older men >60). Pathophysiology: antibodies against postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors (AChR, 85% of generalized MG), muscle-specific kinase (MuSK, 5-10%, more severe bulbar/respiratory involvement), or LRP4. Antibodies cause receptor blockade, internalization, and complement-mediated damage reducing safety factor for neuromuscular transmission. Clinical features: painless weakness worsening with activity, improving with rest; ocular symptoms (ptosis, diplopia—often initial presentation), bulbar symptoms (dysarthria, dysphagia, chewing fatigue), limb weakness (proximal > distal), respiratory muscle involvement (myasthenic crisis). Thymic abnormalities in 75% (hyperplasia in young, thymoma in 10-15%). Myasthenic crisis: respiratory failure requiring intubation, triggered by infection, surgery, medications (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, magnesium).
